Agenda

Talk flow

  • 01 — Timeline : From LameHug to PromptLock

Core question

What changes when ransomware can ask an LLM what to do next?

Timeline

Ransomware 3.0

Classic ransomware

  • Static hardcoded logic
  • Same behavior every run
  • Known signatures — AV catches it
  • Attacker decides everything in advance

PromptLock

  • LLM as a runtime decision engine
  • Logic generated dynamically
  • Adapts to the environment it lands in
  • AI decides what to steal, encrypt, destroy
"To protect, you need to predict."
Context

3 Months That Changed Everything

July 2025
APT28 / LameHug First LLM-powered malware in the wild. Russian state-backed Fancy Bear deploys LameHug against Ukrainian defense targets via HuggingFace API. Delivery: spear-phishing emails targeting government agencies.
Aug. 2025
ESET discovers PromptLock Found on VirusTotal with no known signature match. First documented AI-powered ransomware. ESET publicly disclosed it on August 27, 2025.
Sept. 2025
NYU Tandon claims authorship Academic PoC to demonstrate AI-orchestrated malware risks. Concept went from theoretical to in-the-wild in 60 days.
Timeline — Source

LameHug — July 2025

Ukraine pins AI-powered LameHug attacks on APT28 — July 21, 2025

What made LameHug different

  • Used HuggingFace API to generate shell commands dynamically
  • No static payload — commands varied per target
  • Delivered through phishing emails to government agencies
  • First documented nation-state use of an LLM in malware

This was the proof of concept that the security community feared. PromptLock came one month later — with full ransomware capabilities.

Technical Breakdown

PromptLock Architecture

┌────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ PROMPTLOCK ARCHITECTURE │ ├────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Wrapper written in Go │ │ Compiles for Win / Linux / macOS │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ Static prompts hardcoded in binary │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ Ollama API — localhost:11434 │ │ Model: llama3.2:1b │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ LLM generates Lua scripts at runtime │ │ ├── Recon and file classification │ │ ├── Exfiltration via curl to C2 │ │ └── Encryption — SPECK 128-bit │ └────────────────────────────────────────┘

The malicious logic is never written in advance. Generated fresh on every execution — no two infections produce the same code.

Malicious prompts hardcoded in the Go wrapper
Attack Flow

3-Phase Execution

01 — Recon

  • LLM generates a Lua recon script
  • Scans filesystem for sensitive files
  • Targets: credentials, DB, source code, SSH keys
  • AI classifies and logs high-value files
  • No human decision required

02 — Impact

  • Exfiltration via curl over HTTPS to C2
  • Encryption: SPECK 128-bit
  • Double extortion — steal and encrypt
  • Everything runs in memory

03 — Kill Switch

  • File wipe if ransom not paid
  • One-shot payload, no persistence
  • Different Lua output every run
  • Hash-based IOCs are useless
Infection Vector

How It Gets In — Phishing

Fake Microsoft security update page — delivering promptlock.exe

The scenario

Let's set up a standard phishing delivery the victim downloads and executes what appears to be a legitimate update.

What changes

  • Delivery vector — nothing new
  • What happens after execution — completely different
  • The malware thinks, adapts, decides autonomously
Lab Setup

FlareVM — Detection Environment

Process Monitor — filter on PromptLock process

What I configured

  • FlareVM — isolated VM, network controlled
  • Process Monitor — filtered on promptlock process architecture
  • Wireshark filter: ip.addr == 192.168.192.1 && tcp.port == 11434
  • Snapshot taken before execution

Port 11434 is the default Ollama API port. Every prompt the malware sends to the LLM goes through it. In local mode, this traffic never leaves the host — making it the only internal indicator available.

Demo — Phase 1

Execution — Recon Phase

Process Monitor — PromptLock activity

What Process Monitor shows

  • Reads the Windows hosts file C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts — the malware enumerates the local network configuration to identify the environment.
  • Loads cryptbase.dll — Windows cryptographic library loaded in preparation for the SPECK 128-bit encryption routine.
  • Spawns multiple threads via Thread Create events — the LLM inference process is multithreaded, visible as bursts of thread activity tied to each API call to Ollama.
  • Queries Windows Registry keys related to:
    bam\UserSettings
    WinSock2
    Tcpip
    Session Manager
Demo — Network

Wireshark — LLM Calls Captured

What the capture shows

  • HTTP POST to /api/generate on port 11434
  • Source: victim VM — Destination: LLM host
  • Content-Type: application/json
  • Multiple requests — one per malicious prompt
  • 200 OK responses containing generated Lua code

Each packet is a conversation between the malware and the AI. The malware asks, the AI answers with attack code.

Wireshark — POST /api/generate captured
Demo — Packet Inspection

Inside the Packet

Packet content — LLM prompt and full response

The request

POST /api/generate HTTP/1.1 Host: 192.168.192.1:11434 User-Agent: PromptLock-Ransomware {"model":"llama3.2:1b", "prompt":"Generate a script to scan and identify sensitive files", "stream":false}
  • User-Agent exposes the malware identity
  • LLM responds with a full Python recon script
  • Script targets .ssh, .aws, Documents, config files
  • Executed immediately after reception
Demo — Exfiltration

Double Extortion — AI Decides to Steal

Second prompt captured

After recon, the malware sends a second prompt asking the LLM to generate an exfiltration script. The AI produces code to copy files to a remote server via SSH.

  • Files are stolen before encryption
  • Attacker can leak data even after victim recovers
  • Entirely autonomous — no attacker interaction
  • The LLM decides the exfiltration method
Wireshark — exfiltration prompt in packet 34
Demo — Exfiltration

LLM Generates the Upload Script

LLM response — complete upload script generated

What the LLM produced

  • Full Python script using paramiko and Fernet
  • Connects to attacker server via SSH
  • Encrypts files locally before transfer
  • Uploads via SFTP
  • Generated on the fly — different every run

This is the core problem. The script varies with each infection. There is no static hash to match. Traditional signature-based detection fails here.

Detection Gap

What EDR Sees — And What It Misses

Behavior EDR Detects? What it classifies as
Rapid file encryption High Ransomware behavior
recovery tampering Medium Impact activity
Suspicious process spawning Ollama Medium Unusual child process
Large GGUF model dropped on disk Low–Medium Unknown binary / data file
In-memory prompt logic NO No behavioral alert
RAM spike from local model execution Low Performance anomaly only

EDR will stop the ransomware. It will not understand what it stopped. Post-incident forensics without these IOCs is incomplete.

IOCs

Indicators of Compromise

Filesystem and Process

*.gguf — LLM model file on disk
ollama.exe / ollama — LLM runtime process
target_file_list.log — recon output
note.txt — ransom note
RAM spike 4 to 8 GB — model loaded
HTTP POST /api/generate:11434 — LLM API call

Static Strings in Binary

"You are a Lua code generator"
"gpt-oss:20b" — model name
"Ollama" — API engine
"SPECK" — cipher name
localhost:11434 — local API
User-Agent: PromptLock-Ransomware
Detection — Binary

YARA Rule — Static Detection

rule PromptLock_LLM_Ransomware { meta: description = "Detects PromptLock LLM-embedded ransomware" author = "Othmane Mrichcha" date = "2026" reference = "github.com/othmane-mrichcha/promptlock-research" strings: $s1 = "You are a Lua code generator" ascii $s2 = "gpt-oss:20b" ascii $s3 = "Ollama" ascii nocase $s4 = "SPECK" ascii $s5 = "11434" ascii $s6 = "target_file_list.log" ascii $s7 = "PromptLock-Ransomware" ascii condition: 3 of ($s*) }

When to use it

  • File scanning on ingestion — email gateway, endpoint
  • VirusTotal, threat intelligence platforms
  • Forensic analysis of suspicious binaries

Priority strings

  • $s7 — PromptLock-Ransomware User-Agent — high confidence alone
  • $s1 + $s3 — LLM prompt injection pattern
  • Any 3 of 7 — flag for investigation
Detection — Runtime

Sigma Rules — Process and Network

Rule 1 — Suspicious Ollama execution

detection: selection: Image|endswith: '\ollama.exe' filter_legit: Image|contains: - 'Program Files\Ollama' - 'AppData\Local\Ollama' condition: selection and not filter_legit level: high

Catches Ollama launched from unexpected paths — temp, AppData, user directories.

Rule 2 — Outbound connection port 11434

detection: selection: Initiated: 'true' DestinationPort: 11434 filter_legit: Image|contains: - 'Program Files\Ollama' - 'AppData\Local\Ollama' condition: selection and not filter_legit level: medium

Catches cloud mode — LLM running on attacker server. Correlate with Rule 1 for confirmation.

Detection — Filesystem and HTTP

Sigma Rules — File and Proxy

Rule 3 — GGUF model file outside dev path

detection: selection: TargetFilename|endswith: '.gguf' filter_legit: TargetFilename|contains: - '.ollama\models' - 'Program Files\Ollama' condition: selection and not filter_legit level: high

GGUF files are 4–8 GB LLM weights. No reason to appear outside a dev environment.

Rule 4 — PromptLock User-Agent in proxy logs

detection: selection: cs-user-agent|contains: 'PromptLock-Ransomware' condition: selection level: critical

No false positives expected. One hit triggers immediate IR workflow. This string has no legitimate use case.

Full rules available on GitHub — compatible with Splunk, Elastic, Sentinel, QRadar.

Takeaways

What You Leave With

01

Network monitoring has limits

In local mode, no external traffic is generated. Endpoint monitoring and internal segmentation are the real detection surface.

02

EDR catches the symptom, not the cause

It will stop the encryption. It will not tell you the malware used an AI to decide what to encrypt. Forensics needs these IOCs.

03

Deploy now, before it scales

Deploy the YARA rule on your file scanning pipeline. Push the 4 Sigma rules to your SIEM today. Compatible with Splunk, Elastic, Sentinel, QRadar.

$ git push origin main
